problem_summary: 2020 MCM Problem A (continuous/environmental): predict how two commercially important Scottish fish species (herring and mackerel) will migrate over the next 50 years as sea-surface temperature rises due to global warming, identify when/where small Scottish fishing companies can no longer reach them, and recommend how those companies should adapt their operations (including whether to relocate fishing operations to other countries' waters). This paper (team 2002354) won Outstanding by chaining a temperature forecast into a grid-based migration simulation, then into a fishing-time/profit model, and finally into a concrete two-step business strategy.
models_used: Grey prediction model GM(1,1), enhanced/augmented with exogenous drivers (greenhouse-gas emissions, forest coverage, world population) — used to forecast sea-surface temperature (SST) 50 years ahead from only 40 years (1980-2019) of data, deliberately dropping the anomalous 1970 cold wave

Grey Relational Analysis (degree-of-association) to rank and justify which drivers most influence SST: human factors 0.9988, greenhouse-gas 0.9784, forest area 0.9388, population 0.9132 — used to validate the choice of GM(1,1) exogenous inputs
Ordinary least squares (OLS, B^T B inverse) to estimate the GM(1,1) whitening-equation parameters a and b from the cumulative (1-AGO) sequence
Posterior-variance-ratio C-test (C = S2/S1, ratio of residual variance to original-data variance) for grey-model validation on a held-out set (20 of 225 grid points)

Weighted linear environmental-suitability index E = β1·SST + β2·SSS + β3·BM (sea-surface temperature, sea-surface salinity, bathymetry/sea depth), with expert-assigned weights per species (Herring 0.69/0.21/0.10; Mackerel 0.56/0.25/0.19)
Comfort Zone (CZ) concept: a per-species index band (Herring 4.35-5.51, Mackerel 4.06-6.60) plus a hysteresis threshold (0.1) below which fish do not move

Greedy local-search / cellular-automaton-style migration simulation on a latitude-longitude grid: each year the fish cluster evaluates the self-centered 3x3 neighborhood and steps toward the cell whose E best matches its preferred index, capped at a max migration speed (<=2 grid cells/year)

Three-scenario (best / most-likely / worst) analysis driven by upper/lower confidence bounds on the SST forecast, producing elapsed fishing-time estimates (e.g. herring gone by 2063 most-likely, 2044 worst case)

Spatial fish-abundance model: shoal occupies a circle (radius ~3 lat/long units) with catch density falling off as a normal distribution from the center; fishable only within 120 km of the continental shelf (from Jansen 2012)
Net-profit economic model: NP = Qi·Pi − 0.025·CFR·CoS (catch × price minus per-vessel cost × Scottish share ≈2.5% of EU fleet), plotted as 3-scenario time series to 2070

Cross-border 'resistance coefficient' model: abstracts tariffs, sailing risk, and fishing-agreement limits into a multiplicative cost factor (Norway C=1.2, Iceland C=1.5) to compare staying domestic (Shetland) vs. fishing abroad
key_techniques: Driver-augmented grey forecasting: rather than vanilla GM(1,1), they first ran grey relational analysis to identify GHG/forest/population as SST drivers, then justified GM(1,1) as the right tool for the data-scarce 40-year window — turning a weakness (little data) into a stated strength
Deliberate data cleaning with justification: explicitly excluded the 1970 global cold wave to avoid biasing the trend, and used 1980-2019 only — shows judgment, not just blind fitting

Reducing migration to a tractable greedy grid search with a max-speed cap and a comfort-zone hysteresis band, which prevents physically implausible jumps and oscillation — a clean, interpretable surrogate for a hard ecological process
Held-out validation: predicted 225 grid points, reserved 20 as a validation set, reported the C-test ratio (0.55, 'acceptable') and was honest that error grows in later years

Sensitivity analysis applied uniformly across every model stage (±0.1% on β1/β2/β3/CZ, on fishing radius, and on resistance coefficients), each reporting <0.5% positional / <0.3-0.4% quantity change to argue robustness

End-to-end model chaining: SST forecast → environmental index → migration trajectory → reachable fishing time → catch quantity → net profit → relocation decision, so every downstream answer is traceable to the temperature model

Translating quantitative output into an actionable, staged business plan: the 'two-step' strategy (2020-2040 domestic move to Shetland + 50% long-endurance paired boats; 2040-2070 conditional cross-border move to Norway/Iceland based on balance sheets)

Audience-tailored deliverable: the requested magazine column ('Hook Line and Sinker') is written in a deliberately journalistic, persuasive register with idioms and rhetorical hooks, distinct from the technical body
why_it_won: "Judges reward a complete, traceable modeling chain that actually answers every sub-question, plus communication, and this paper delivers both. It builds a single causal spine — temperature drivers → SST → species-specific suitability index → year-by-year grid migration → reachable catch → company profit → relocation choice — so no question is answered in isolation and every economic conclusion is anchored in the physical forecast. It makes principled modeling choices (excluding the 1970 cold wave, choosing grey prediction precisely because data is scarce, justifying drivers via relational analysis), validates with a held-out C-test, and runs a uniform sensitivity analysis at every stage. Critically it converts numbers into concrete, defensible decisions (move to Shetland, then conditionally to Norway for herring / Iceland for mackerel, with a resistance-coefficient cost comparison and a phased 20%-per-5-years asset transfer). The migration maps and 3-scenario profit curves give judges immediate visual takeaways, and the standalone magazine article nails the explicit communication deliverable in an engaging voice."
section_highlights: "Summary is decision-first: it states the migration outcome (herring → NE near Norway, mackerel → NW near Iceland), the dated extinction-from-fishery years (herring 0 by 2063 most-likely, 2044 worst; mackerel 2068 worst), and the two-step recommendation up front. Each technical subsection ends with an explicit 'Strength and Weakness' box, which signals self-awareness to judges and structures the whole paper. Notation table and assumptions are front-loaded (including the clever assumption that abstracts cross-border friction into a single resistance coefficient, foreshadowing Section 7). Figures carry the argument: migration trajectory maps with dated waypoints (2041/2045/2070), 3-scenario SST and net-profit line charts, and the Shetland-vs-Iceland / Shetland-vs-Norway profit comparison that visually justifies going abroad. Section 8 'Articles for Hook Line and Sinker' is a separate, rhetorically rich magazine column aimed at fishermen — fulfilling the problem's explicit communication ask rather than reusing the technical text."
pitfalls_or_limitations: The held-out validation is quietly poor: Table 3 shows predicted SST diverging badly from actual by the late years (2019 actual 3.83 vs predicted 7.73, residual ~−3.9°C) and C=0.55 is only 'qualified/acceptable', not good — yet 50-year projections are then treated as reliable. GM(1,1) is fundamentally a smooth-exponential extrapolator and over-warms.

The β weights for the suitability index and the comfort-zone bands are 'expert-assigned' with no fitting/calibration to actual catch data, so the core migration driver rests on hand-picked constants. The species index physically mixes SST, salinity, and depth on one linear scale without unit/normalization detail.

The sensitivity analysis is performed only at ±0.1% perturbations — an extremely narrow band almost guaranteed to show 'no significant change'. It demonstrates local numerical stability, not robustness to the real uncertainty in weights, the SST forecast, or the abundance assumptions. This is a common MCM over-claim.

Assumptions 2 and 3 hard-freeze salinity and depth for 50 years and cap migration at 2 grid cells/year with no biological justification beyond convenience; reproduction, predators, food, and currents are explicitly excluded (acknowledged but not bounded).

The resistance coefficients (Norway 1.2, Iceland 1.5) and the Scottish fleet share (2.5% of EU) are essentially asserted from sparse sources; the whole cross-border profit comparison hinges on these unvalidated multipliers.

Some figure/data tables in the extracted markdown are clearly OCR-garbled (3D plots, CFR table), and several SST values (e.g. −1000 ice flags, −1.8 placeholder rows) suggest the raw grid had heavy missing/sentinel data that the pipeline may not have fully handled.
reusable_patterns: Causal-chain architecture: pick one driver variable (here SST), forecast it, then make every subsequent model a deterministic function of that forecast so all answers are traceable and mutually consistent. Reuse for any 'predict X then decide Y' MCM problem.

Grey GM(1,1) as the go-to forecaster when you have a short (~20-40 point) time series and need long-horizon projection; pair it with grey relational analysis to justify exogenous drivers and a posterior-variance C-test plus a held-out set for validation.

Weighted linear suitability index E = Σ βi·factor_i with a per-entity preferred range + a hysteresis 'comfort zone' threshold to model behavioral/threshold responses — directly reusable for habitat, location, or choice models.
Greedy 3x3-neighborhood grid stepping with a max-speed cap as a cheap, interpretable substitute for a full agent-based or PDE migration/diffusion model.
Best/most-likely/worst scenario framing by taking upper/lower bounds of a forecast and propagating them through downstream models — gives judges a clean risk picture and dated outcomes.

Abstracting messy real-world frictions (tariffs, regulations, weather risk) into a single multiplicative 'resistance coefficient', then doing sensitivity on it, to keep an economic comparison tractable.
Always close each model subsection with an explicit Strength/Weakness box, and front-load a decision-first Summary that names concrete dated outcomes and the final recommendation.
Write the required communication deliverable (memo/letter/article) as a genuinely separate, audience-tuned document in a different register — do not just paste the technical conclusions.
Caveat to copy carefully: run sensitivity analysis over realistically wide perturbation ranges (and on the most uncertain parameters), not a token ±0.1%, or judges will see through it.
